title: Algorithm Engineering
description: How real hardware shapes fast code — pipelines, caches and memory.

lectures.title: Lecture 01 · What is Algorithm Engineering?
lectures.description: Why NP-hard doesn't mean unsolvable — and why measuring is hard.

cards.question: Name four typical domains where organizations must solve hard optimization problems, with one concrete task each.
cards.answer: Shift scheduling & task assignment — building a nurse roster. Logistics — routing a delivery fleet. Energy networks — planning grid capacity and dispatch. Production planning — car sequencing, ordering cars on a conveyor so no station is overloaded ('at most 2 sunroofs in 5'). Nearly all of these are NP-hard, yet industry solves them every day.

cards.question: Why is benchmarking optimization algorithms fundamentally harder than benchmarking a sorting algorithm?
cards.answer: Sorting has one clean size axis and one target (time). Optimization has neither. In the nurse rostering example the winner flips between instances and flips depending on when you stop the clock — there is no single 'fastest' solver. NP-hard runtimes vary by orders of magnitude, no single measure captures instance hardness, and you are really comparing two things at once: runtime and solution quality.

lectures.title: Lecture 02 · CPU Architecture 1
lectures.description: Flip through the pipeline — branching, memory & caches.

cards.question: Why care how the CPU works if your algorithm's Big-O already looks good?
cards.answer: Because constant factors matter in real life. Two programs with the same Big-O can differ hugely in speed, and misunderstanding the CPU can hide an enormous constant factor — a 'smart' Python program can lose to a well-tuned C++ one.

cards.question: What is the CPU 'pipeline', in plain terms?
cards.answer: An assembly line for instructions. Different instructions sit at different stages (fetch, decode, execute…) at the same time, so no stage sits idle.

cards.question: What does 'superscalar' mean?
cards.answer: The CPU can work on several independent instructions at once — like having 4 workers on the line instead of 1.

cards.question: What is instruction-level parallelism (ILP)?
cards.answer: The parallelism hidden inside a single instruction stream: independent instructions that can be executed at the same time even though you wrote them in sequence. The CPU harvests it with superscalar issue (several instructions per cycle), out-of-order execution and speculation. ILP is limited by dependencies — if each instruction needs the previous one's result, there is nothing to overlap and the wide machine sits idle.

cards.question: What is 'out-of-order' execution?
cards.answer: If a later instruction is ready while an earlier one is stuck, the ready one can jump the queue. The final results still come out in the correct order.

cards.question: What is 'speculation' in a CPU?
cards.answer: The CPU guesses what's coming next and starts early, before it's fully sure. If the guess was wrong, it throws that work away.

cards.question: A thread must behave as if it ran one instruction after another. Which effects may still differ because of microarchitectural tricks?
cards.answer: The CPU must preserve the architectural state exactly: general-purpose register contents and memory contents always match the sequential program. What it does not promise is anything microarchitectural: execution time, branch predictor state, and cache contents may all differ. That gap is the whole point — it is where the performance comes from (and, incidentally, where side-channel attacks like Spectre live).

cards.question: What is latency?
cards.answer: How long one instruction takes from start to finish. (Like: how long a single pizza takes to bake.)

cards.question: What is throughput?
cards.answer: How many instructions finish per unit of time at full speed. (Like: with many ovens, a fresh pizza comes out every few minutes.)

cards.question: Does the pipeline make a single instruction finish faster?
cards.answer: No — latency stays about the same. What it improves is throughput: many instructions finish in quick succession.

cards.question: Why are branches a problem for the pipeline?
cards.answer: The CPU races ahead and reaches the fork before it knows the answer, so it doesn't know which instructions to load next.

cards.question: How does the CPU cope with not knowing which way a branch goes?
cards.answer: It predicts (guesses) the outcome and speculatively runs ahead based on that guess.

cards.question: What happens when a prediction is right vs wrong?
cards.answer: Right → no time wasted, smooth flow. Wrong (misprediction) → it throws away the speculative work and restarts — an expensive stall.

cards.question: Which branches are cheap and which are slow?
cards.answer: Predictable branches are basically free. Random, coin-flip branches inside a hot loop cause massive slowdowns.

cards.question: How can the CPU predict branches so well?
cards.answer: It spots patterns — 'taken the last 100 times', repeating sequences — and learns from nearby branches. On normal code it's right over 95% of the time.

cards.question: What's the main trick to fix an unpredictable branch?
cards.answer: Branchless code — get the answer with math / conditional moves instead of an 'if', so there's no branch to mispredict.

cards.question: Why not just make everything branchless?
cards.answer: Well-predicted branches are already cheap, and branchless code often does extra work (computes both paths). It only helps when both paths are cheap AND the branch is truly unpredictable.

cards.question: What are caches, and why do they exist?
cards.answer: Small, very fast memory right next to the CPU. They hold data you're likely to need so the CPU doesn't wait on slow RAM.

cards.question: How do the cache levels (L1, L2, L3, RAM) compare?
cards.answer: Closer to the CPU = faster but smaller. L1 is tiny and fastest; RAM is huge but slowest. Goal: keep needed data in the fast, close caches.

cards.question: When the CPU asks for one value, what does the cache actually fetch?
cards.answer: A whole cache line — a chunk of neighboring data (~16 ints), not just the one value — betting you'll want the neighbors soon.

cards.question: What is spatial locality?
cards.answer: If you use one piece of data, you'll probably use the data right next to it soon (like reading left to right).

cards.question: What is temporal locality?
cards.answer: If you use a piece of data, you'll probably use that same data again soon.

cards.question: What two general principles make caching work at all?
cards.answer: Temporal locality — if you touch a piece of data you will probably touch it again soon. Spatial locality — if you touch a piece of data you will probably touch the data next to it soon. Without both, a small fast memory could not predict what to keep, and a cache line (fetching the neighbours too) would be wasted work.

cards.question: What is a prefetcher?
cards.answer: A helper that spots predictable access patterns (like scanning straight through memory) and loads the data early, before you ask.

cards.question: In the matrix experiment, why is reading row-by-row fast?
cards.answer: Data is stored row by row, so reading in that order fully uses each fetched cache line before moving on — no waste.

cards.question: Why is reading a matrix column-by-column slow?
cards.answer: Each jump lands in a different cache line, so the CPU fetches a whole chunk but uses only one value and throws the rest away — over and over.

cards.question: What is 'striding', and why can it hurt?
cards.answer: Jumping through memory in big steps instead of neighboring items. Big strides waste most of each cache line and stop the prefetcher from guessing the pattern.

cards.question: Which data structures are cache-friendly vs not?
cards.answer: Packed ones (arrays / vectors) are great. Scattered, pointer-chasing ones (linked lists, tree maps) are usually slow because the CPU chases data all over memory.

cards.question: What can the compiler optimize for you automatically?
cards.answer: Low-level tricks — simple branchless code, swapping slow operations for fast ones, removing repeated work, vectorizing loops.

cards.question: What can the compiler NOT do for you?
cards.answer: The big decisions — it won't change your data structure, your memory layout, or your algorithm. That's your job.

cards.question: What is the optimization hierarchy, in order?
cards.answer: 1) Correctness first, 2) pick the right algorithm, 3) pick cache-friendly data structures & layout, 4) then measure and fine-tune things like branch prediction.

cards.question: What's the golden rule before optimizing anything?
cards.answer: Measure first. The CPU does so much clever stuff that your guesses about what's slow are often wrong — let real numbers guide you. (And never measure an unoptimized build.)

lectures.title: Lecture 03 · CPU Architecture 2
lectures.description: Optimizing for real hardware — data layout, SIMD & parallelism.

cards.question: AoS vs SoA — what's the difference?
cards.answer: AoS (Array of Structures) keeps each object's fields together (cleaner code). SoA (Structure of Arrays) stores separate lists per field (often faster).

cards.question: Why is SoA often faster than AoS?
cards.answer: Memory is read in chunks (cache lines). Packing needed values together means each chunk is full of useful data instead of skipped-over junk (striding).

cards.question: When is AoS perfectly fine?
cards.answer: When you use <mark>most fields</mark> of each object anyway — then nothing in the cache line is wasted.

cards.question: Why are new/malloc calls a hidden performance trap?
cards.answer: Allocating is <mark>far more expensive</mark> than normal operations — like ordering a new box from the store every time instead of reusing one.

cards.question: How do you fix allocating inside a hot loop?
cards.answer: Allocate once before the loop and reuse it (e.g. clear() a vector to keep its buffer), or reserve() capacity / use an object pool.

cards.question: What are 'hidden allocations'?
cards.answer: Accidental copies of containers or temporaries — often because C++ passes objects by value by default. Profile to catch them.

cards.question: What does SIMD mean and do?
cards.answer: Single Instruction, Multiple Data — one instruction applies the same operation to many values at once, like a cashier with 8 hands scanning 8 items in one beep.

cards.question: Should you usually write SIMD by hand?
cards.answer: No — it's complex and platform-dependent. Let the compiler do it (auto-vectorization); go manual only as a last resort after measuring.

cards.question: Most common reason the compiler doesn't vectorize?
cards.answer: Missing optimization flags — you need -O2/-O3 turned on (and sometimes flags telling it the hardware, e.g. AVX2, is available).

cards.question: What code patterns block vectorization?
cards.answer: Messy control flow (if/else, non-inlined calls), complex access patterns, and inter-iteration dependencies.

cards.question: What is an inter-iteration dependency?
cards.answer: When a step needs the previous step's result (a chain), so steps can't run at the same time. Example: v[i] = v[i-1]*w[i] is blocked.

cards.question: Compilers split a sum into several partial sums. Why does breaking the dependency chain help even without vectorization?
cards.answer: One accumulator means every add must wait for the previous add's result, so the loop runs at one add per add-latency (say 4 cycles) — the pipeline's other slots sit empty. Several partial sums are independent, so they can be in flight at once and the loop runs at the add's throughput instead (several per cycle). You are converting a latency-bound chain into instruction-level parallelism; vectorization is a separate, additional win on top.

cards.question: Why can float sums be hard to vectorize?
cards.answer: Reordering float additions can slightly change the result, so the compiler won't reorder unless allowed. Float add is commutative but not strictly associative.

cards.question: std::accumulate vs std::reduce for summing floats?
cards.answer: accumulate is strict left-to-right (can't reorder → no SIMD). reduce may reorder (→ can vectorize).

cards.question: What is memory alignment and why care?
cards.answer: Data should sit at a <mark>'nice' address</mark> (like parking a bus only in a bus-shaped spot). Misalignment hampers vectorization and causes slow loads that split cache lines.

cards.question: Practical trick to shrink a struct?
cards.answer: Reorder fields (big types first) to reduce padding — the same data can go from 40 bytes to 24 bytes for free.

cards.question: Why do we need parallelization now?
cards.answer: Per-core speed and frequency stopped growing (~2005); CPUs add more cores instead, so you must use many at once.

cards.question: What does Amdahl's Law tell us?
cards.answer: Only the parallelizable part speeds up; the <mark>part that must run alone caps total speedup</mark> — even with infinite cores. (Chefs share chopping, but one solo cake limits the meal.)

cards.question: The two fundamental hurdles of parallelization?
cards.answer: Synchronization (coordinating shared data) and shared resources (memory bandwidth, power, heat, network).

cards.question: What is the memory bandwidth limit?
cards.answer: A <mark>shared pipe to memory</mark>. Once it's full, more threads don't help — like many people sharing one water fountain.

cards.question: When is a task likely bandwidth-limited?
cards.answer: Reading lots of memory once, in a predictable linear scan, more data than cache, with light computation. Then a few threads already max it out.

cards.question: Best strategies to reduce synchronization?
cards.answer: Have each thread <mark>work its own chunk and combine at the end</mark>; keep critical sections short; hand out big-enough tasks.

cards.question: What is false sharing?
cards.answer: Threads write to different variables that share one cache line, so each write forces the others to refresh — like two people sharing one notebook page.

cards.question: Does false sharing cause wrong answers, and how do you fix it?
cards.answer: No — results are correct, just secretly slow. Fix by putting each thread's data on its own cache line (e.g. alignas(64)).

cards.question: Why can CPU utilization mislead you?
cards.answer: Busy-waiting (spinning) looks like 100% usage but does no useful work. Prefer 'effective time' plus wait/spin time.

cards.question: Low-level vs high-level parallelism?
cards.answer: Low-level = parallelize one task (parallel sum/sort). High-level = run whole different strategies at once (many seeds, different algorithms) — usually better.

lectures.title: Tutorial 01 · Performance Engineering in Practice
lectures.description: Find the bottleneck before you touch it — Amdahl, profilers, counters.

cards.question: State Amdahl's Law and explain what it implies for where you spend optimization effort.
cards.answer: If a fraction p of the runtime can be sped up by a factor s, then speedup = 1 / ((1 − p) + p/s). The untouched (1 − p) is a hard ceiling — making a routine that is 5% of runtime infinitely fast still buys you only 5%. So: speed up the part that dominates runtime, not the part that looks embarrassing.

cards.question: Profiling tools fall into two big families. Name both, describe each mechanism, and give the main trade-off.
cards.answer: Tracing / instrumentation — records every call or event (e.g. cProfile): exact call counts, but high overhead that can distort the very thing you measure. Sampling — interrupts the program periodically and reads the program counter (e.g. Scalene, perf record): only a statistical estimate, but low overhead. The trade-off is exactness vs. overhead and distortion.

cards.question: Which hardware-counter metrics would you examine in a micro-benchmark, and what does each diagnose?
cards.answer: cycles + instructions → IPC, how well the pipeline is being fed. cache-references / cache-misses → locality and data layout. branches / branch-misses → unpredictable conditionals. task-clock vs. wall time → whether you are blocked rather than slow. Collected with perf stat, which classifies the bottleneck; perf record then locates it in the code.

cards.question: Why does a single hardware counter rarely diagnose anything — what do you read instead?
cards.answer: Read the signature across counters, not one number. Compute-bound = high IPC + low miss rate (blame the instruction count / algorithm). Memory-bound = low IPC + miss pressure (blame locality and layout). Branch-heavy = high branch-miss % on hot code. The trap: task-clock ≪ wall time means the program is waiting, not computing — sampling harder won't help, you need off-CPU tools.

lectures.title: Lecture 04 · Fundamental Data Structures
lectures.description: Why the data structure — not the Big-O — decides real speed.

cards.question: What's the default linear structure, and why?
cards.answer: std::vector. It's contiguous, predictable, and cache-friendly — reach for something else only when the workload clearly demands it.

cards.question: Why is std::list usually much slower than std::vector?
cards.answer: A vector is contiguous, so the prefetcher streams ahead (one fetch → 8 elements). A list scatters nodes, so every hop risks a main-memory miss (~100× a cache hit).

cards.question: When does std::list actually win? (all three at once)
cards.answer: 1) Many splices, few traversals; 2) iterator stability (pointers must survive edits elsewhere); 3) elements expensive to move. If you can't name all three → use vector.

cards.question: What about std::deque?
cards.answer: Avoid it. It's a map of pointers to blocks — every iterator step risks crossing a block boundary. Prefer a vector with a read index as a queue (contiguous, one allocation).

cards.question: What is small-buffer optimization?
cards.answer: Store a few elements inside the object itself (on the stack), avoiding a heap allocation while small. Use absl::InlinedVector, boost::small_vector, llvm::SmallVector.

cards.question: Before using a hash map, which cases have a better tool?
cards.answer: Dense integer keys → index a std::vector directly. Ordered queries → sorted container. Tiny N (≲100) → sorted vector + linear scan (cache beats asymptotics).

cards.question: How would you store an attribute keyed by node ID (IDs are incremental ints from 0, deletions rare, IDs never reused)?
cards.answer: A flat std::vector<T> indexed directly by node ID — no hashing, no allocation per key, one cache line per access. The IDs are already a dense perfect index, so a hash map only adds overhead. Deletions are rare and IDs are never reused, so you simply leave a hole (a tombstone / validity flag, or a sentinel value) rather than compacting; the vector grows with new nodes and never needs to shrink. Wasted slots cost memory only, and only if deletions ever become common.

cards.question: Why avoid std::unordered_map?
cards.answer: The standard mandates chaining — it's a linked list in disguise. Every insert allocates a node; every lookup chases a pointer. ~2× slower on hits, 3–4× on misses.

cards.question: What is open addressing and why is it faster?
cards.answer: Store (key,value) inline in one array; collisions probe adjacent slots. One allocation, and a hit/miss usually stays in the same cache line. Downside: wide values → fewer slots per line.

cards.question: What clever trick do Swiss tables add?
cards.answer: They split tiny metadata from the data. A single SIMD instruction checks 16 slot-tags at once; only matches pay to read the full key-value pair — fast regardless of value width.

cards.question: Default hash map + main tuning knobs?
cards.answer: Use ankerl::unordered_dense, absl::flat_hash_map, or boost::unordered_flat_map. reserve() before bulk inserts, leave load factor alone, and use a good hash for non-trivial keys.

cards.question: When do you actually need a sorted container?
cards.answer: Only when order is part of the query: range queries, predecessor/successor, ordered iteration, order statistics, DB indexes. If it's just point lookup → hash map.

cards.question: Default sorted structure, and its one weakness?
cards.answer: Sorted std::vector + std::lower_bound — build once, binary-search many. Weakness: mid-insert is O(N) (one insert into a million-key vector shifts megabytes).

cards.question: Why avoid std::map?
cards.answer: It's a red-black tree with per-node malloc — basically a std::list with a sort order, plus rebalancing on every insert. Slower than absl::btree_map on every workload.

cards.question: For balanced trees, where does the real cost come from?
cards.answer: From tree height, not node work. A million-key binary tree is ~20 levels = up to 20 cache misses per lookup. Fix: widen the branching factor to shrink height.

cards.question: What makes a B-tree / B+ tree fast?
cards.answer: Each node is sized to a cache line (order chosen so one node = one cache line, typically dozens of keys) → one cache miss per level instead of one per comparison, and the height collapses to log_B N. The trade-off in choosing the order: a larger node means fewer levels (fewer misses) but more linear scanning inside a node and more data to shift on insert — so you grow it until a node stops fitting the cache line. B+ trees additionally keep all values in linked leaves, so a range query is one descent + a linear leaf walk — why every database uses them.

cards.question: Best choice for a mutation-heavy ordered map?
cards.answer: absl::btree_map — a drop-in for std::map, ~3× faster lookup, per-op cost bounded by log N, and no per-node allocation.

cards.question: When do you actually need a priority queue?
cards.answer: When you pop and keep inserting in a loop (event sim, Dijkstra/A*, top-k, scheduling, branch-and-bound). If you just need order once at the end → std::sort.

cards.question: What is a binary heap, physically?
cards.answer: An implicit tree stored in a flat array (std::vector) — parent/child are just index arithmetic. No pointers, no per-node allocations, cache-friendly.

cards.question: Cheap way to speed up a large heap?
cards.answer: Use a d-ary heap (d = 4 or 8): each node has d children instead of 2. Same array + index math, the d children sit in one cache line, and height drops.

cards.question: How do you 'change a key' in a heap (which it can't do)?
cards.answer: Push a new entry instead (lazy deletion): on pop, skip stale entries whose priority no longer matches. This also kills the last reason to use a Fibonacci heap (cache-hostile in practice).

cards.question: What is union-find and what two tricks make it fast?
cards.answer: Maintains a partition under find(x) and unite(x,y) using just a parent array. Tricks: union by rank (attach shorter tree under taller) + path compression (re-parent nodes to the root during find).

cards.question: What's its real-world cost?
cards.answer: Amortized inverse-Ackermann — which is ≤ 4 for any real input, so treat both ops as effectively O(1). Constant factor is dominated by one hot cache line (the parent array).

cards.question: What's the default graph representation?
cards.answer: Compressed Sparse Row (CSR) — two flat arrays (offsets + targets). Neighbors are contiguous, so traversal streams at memory bandwidth. CSR is to graphs what vector is to lists.

cards.question: Why avoid vector<vector<int>> for a long-lived graph?
cards.answer: Each vertex's neighbors are a separate heap allocation → one cache miss per vertex before you read a single edge. Same pathology as std::list.

cards.question: When is an adjacency matrix the right call?
cards.answer: For small, dense graphs, when edge-existence queries dominate, or when the algorithm is linear algebra (SpMV, matrix powers). Costs V² memory always.

cards.question: How do you pick a spatial structure?
cards.answer: By the query, not the data. Range → grid/R-tree; nearest-neighbor → kd-tree; ray → BVH; collision → BVH/grid. The whole win comes from pruning almost everything.

cards.question: Default spatial structure, and kd-tree's big caveat?
cards.answer: Uniform grid — simple, near-O(1) for near-neighbor queries on roughly uniform points. kd-trees work in low dimensions but degrade to linear scan (~20D) — the curse of dimensionality.

cards.question: Bloom filter — what and what's the catch?
cards.answer: Answers “have I seen it?” in a tiny bit array using k hashes. False positives possible, but never false negatives. ~10 bits/element ≈ 1% error is the usual default.

cards.question: Count-Min sketch vs HyperLogLog — one line each?
cards.answer: Count-Min: approximate frequencies (“how often?”), always an overcount, never under. HyperLogLog: approximate distinct count (“how many unique?”) in kilobytes — powers COUNT(DISTINCT).

lectures.title: Lecture 05 · Optimization Fundamentals
lectures.description: Eight ways to solve optimization problems, all taught through the Knapsack puzzle.

cards.question: In plain words, what is the Knapsack problem?
cards.answer: You have a backpack with a weight limit and treasures that each have a weight and a value — pick items to get the highest total value without going over the limit.

cards.question: What is the core idea of dynamic programming?
cards.answer: Solve many small subproblems first, store their answers, and reuse them to build up the solution to the big problem.

cards.question: For each item, what are the only two choices dynamic programming compares?
cards.answer: Either skip the item (keep the previous best) or take the item (gain its value but use up bag space) — keep whichever is better.

cards.question: Is dynamic programming usually the best choice in practice?
cards.answer: No — the tables often become too big to be practical. It's common in theory but rarely the best real-world choice; Knapsack is a lucky exception.

cards.question: How does the greedy Knapsack algorithm decide what to pack?
cards.answer: Sort items by value per weight (efficiency) and take them in that order as long as they still fit.

cards.question: Why is the greedy result useful even though it can be imperfect?
cards.answer: It's fast and always a valid, real packing, so it gives a lower bound — a guaranteed "you can do at least this well" (a floor).

cards.question: What extra power does the fractional Knapsack give you?
cards.answer: You may take fractions of items (slice them) to fill the bag exactly to the brim.

cards.question: Why does the fractional solution give an upper bound?
cards.answer: Slicing adds freedom, so it can only be as good or better than the real puzzle — its value is a ceiling the true answer can never beat.

cards.question: What does it mean to "relax" a problem?
cards.answer: A relaxation is an easier version of the problem (like allowing fractions) that gives a useful bound on the real answer.

cards.question: How can all possible Knapsack solutions be pictured?
cards.answer: As a decision tree of yes/no choices — each node is a partial plan and each edge is one "take it / leave it" decision.

cards.question: What is the key move in backtracking?
cards.answer: If a partial plan is already too heavy, stop and discard that whole branch — nothing below it can be valid.

cards.question: What is constraint propagation?
cards.answer: Using logic to auto-decide forced choices before branching — working out the obvious consequences of what you've already picked.

cards.question: Give an example of a forced choice in Knapsack propagation.
cards.answer: If an item can't possibly fit, automatically leave it; if leftover items are free value and all fit, automatically take them.

cards.question: How does branch & bound go beyond backtracking?
cards.answer: It also discards branches that are possible but can't beat the best answer found so far — not just impossible ones.

cards.question: What two values does branch & bound track in order to prune?
cards.answer: A lower bound (best real answer so far) and each branch's upper bound (best it could possibly reach); if the branch's ceiling can't beat your record, drop it.

cards.question: Bound approach A is fast but weak; B is slow but tighter. Discuss the trade-off — why can either win?
cards.answer: Total time ≈ (nodes explored) × (cost per node), and the two approaches move these in opposite directions. A (fast, weak) rips through many nodes per second, but prunes less, so the tree stays large — it wins when the weak bound is almost as good, or when the instance is easy enough that raw node throughput dominates. B (slow, tight) pays much more per node but cuts whole subtrees, and the saving is exponential in the tree — it wins when the tighter bound actually prunes branches A would explore, especially deep in a hard instance and when proving optimality. Neither dominates: it depends on whether the extra pruning outweighs the extra cost per node, which is why solvers hedge (cheap bound at every node, expensive bound only at promising ones).

cards.question: When do you reach for metaheuristics?
cards.answer: When the problem is too big or too nasty for exact methods to finish — they find a really good answer without promising it's perfect.

cards.question: What is the exploration vs. exploitation trade-off?
cards.answer: Exploration roams widely to scout new areas, while exploitation polishes what you already have; a good strategy explores first, then intensifies.

cards.question: What single idea unifies all of these methods?
cards.answer: They are all smarter ways to search the tree of take-it/leave-it choices, trapping the answer between a floor and a ceiling.

lectures.title: Tutorial 02 · Declarative Modeling
lectures.description: The five-component recipe: turn a paragraph of prose into a model.

cards.question: Name the five components of an optimization model in order — the question each answers and the mathematical object it becomes.
cards.answer: 1. Entities — what objects does the problem talk about? → sets. 2. Parameters — what data is known up front? → constants indexed by those sets. 3. Variables — what is the solver allowed to choose? → unknowns with a domain. 4. Constraints — what makes a choice valid? → relations on the variables. 5. Objective — how do we rank the feasible ones? → a function to minimise or maximise. It is a recurring guide, not a one-pass checklist — a new constraint may demand a new variable.

cards.question: Apply the five-component recipe: assign every parcel to exactly one van without exceeding van capacity, minimising handling cost.
cards.answer: Entities: parcels P, vans V. Parameters: weight wp, capacity Cv, handling cost cpv. Variables: xpv ∈ {0,1} — parcel p is loaded into van v. Constraints: Σv xpv = 1 for every p ('exactly one of its available vans'); Σp wp·xpv ≤ Cv for every v ('must not exceed'). Objective: min Σp,v cpv·xpv.

lectures.title: Lecture 06 · Bill Cook
lectures.description: No Lecture. Bill Cook was a guest speaker.

lectures.title: Lecture 07 · LP & MIP
lectures.description: How solvers use easy linear programs to crack hard integer problems.

cards.question: What is Linear Programming (LP) and what makes it the 'easy' version?
cards.answer: Maximizing a goal under straight-line rules where answers may be fractions. It's easy because computers can solve it quickly.

cards.question: What is Mixed Integer Programming (MIP) and why is it the 'hard' version?
cards.answer: An LP where some variables must be whole numbers. That single requirement makes it genuinely hard to solve.

cards.question: What does it mean that 'LP is in P'?
cards.answer: Computers can solve LP quickly (in polynomial time), even for large problems, in both theory and practice.

cards.question: Write down the general form of a minimization LP.
cards.answer: min cᵀx subject to Ax ≥ b, x ≥ 0 — x is the vector of decision variables, c the cost coefficients, A the constraint matrix and b the right-hand sides. Every relation is linear and x is continuous; that is exactly what puts LP in P.

cards.question: What mainly determines how long an LP takes to solve?
cards.answer: The number of variables, the number of constraints, and how sparse the problem is (how few non-zero coefficients).

cards.question: How does the Simplex algorithm find the answer?
cards.answer: It walks along the edges of the shape of valid answers, checking corners one at a time.

cards.question: What are Simplex's strengths and weaknesses?
cards.answer: Strengths: many cheap steps, great at warm starts, clean solutions. Weaknesses: not proven fast, hard to parallelize.

cards.question: How does the Barrier (Interior Point) method find the answer?
cards.answer: It floats through the middle of the shape and homes in on the answer, instead of crawling along edges.

cards.question: What are Barrier's strengths and weaknesses?
cards.answer: Strengths: proven fast, parallelizable, fewer steps. Weaknesses: each step is expensive, bad at warm starts.

cards.question: What makes PDLP (PDHG) special among LP solvers?
cards.answer: It takes many cheap steps and can even use GPUs, making it very fast to moderate precision.

cards.question: What is a 'warm start' and why does it matter?
cards.answer: Reusing a previous solution after a small change, so you only need a few extra steps instead of solving from scratch.

cards.question: What is an Integer Program vs. a Binary Program?
cards.answer: Integer Program: all variables are whole numbers. Binary Program: variables are only 0 or 1 (yes/no switches).

cards.question: How hard are MIP, IP, and BP as problem classes?
cards.answer: They are all NP-complete — the 'really tough' category of problems.

cards.question: Why does 'whole numbers only' make a problem so hard?
cards.answer: Valid answers become scattered dots instead of a smooth shape, so you can't just slide to the best one.

cards.question: What is the Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP)?
cards.answer: Visit every city exactly once and return home using the shortest total route.

cards.question: How would you model the TSP as a MIP?
cards.answer: DFJ (undirected): one binary xₑ per edge, minimise Σ cₑ·xₑ, with degree constraints Σe ∈ δ(i) xₑ = 2 for every city, plus subtour elimination Σe ∈ δ(S) xₑ ≥ 2 for every ∅ ⊊ S ⊊ V. Degrees alone allow a set of disjoint cycles; the cut constraints force a single tour. There are 2ⁿ−2 of them, so they are added lazily — separation is a min cut, and any cut of value < 2 is a violated inequality. MTZ (directed) is the writable alternative (position variables uᵢ with a Big-M) but its relaxation is weak.

cards.question: What are lazy constraints, and where did we use them?
cards.answer: A constraint family too large to write down is left out of the model entirely. You solve, inspect the solution, and add back only the constraints it actually violates, repeating until none are. It is not proven that few suffice — but in practice they do. Example: TSP subtour elimination, where we start with no subtour constraints and add one each time the LP hands back disjoint cycles.

cards.question: In the treasure-hunt view of solving, what is the Upper Bound (UB)?
cards.answer: The best real, complete route found so far — proof that a route this good exists.

cards.question: In the treasure-hunt view of solving, what is the Lower Bound (relaxation)?
cards.answer: A dream estimate from the easy LP saying no route could possibly be better than this.

cards.question: When do you know you've found the optimal answer?
cards.answer: When the upper bound and lower bound meet at the same value — the answer is then proven best.

cards.question: What problem do cutting planes solve?
cards.answer: The LP gives a fractional answer (like 'use 0.5 of a road') that can't be used in reality.

cards.question: What is a cutting plane, in one idea?
cards.answer: A new rule that slices off the fractional answer without removing any valid whole-number answer.

cards.question: What three properties must a good cutting plane have?
cards.answer: It's linear, satisfied by all valid integer solutions, and violated by the current fractional solution.

cards.question: What is the 'separation problem'?
cards.answer: The task of finding a good cutting plane; usually solved by a heuristic that can sometimes fail.

cards.question: What does it mean to 'branch' on a variable?
cards.answer: Take a variable stuck at a fraction and split into two what-if worlds (e.g. round it down vs. round it up), solving each.

cards.question: What is 'pruning' and why is it so powerful?
cards.answer: Throwing away a branch whose best-possible value is already worse than a real answer you have, avoiding huge amounts of work.

cards.question: What is Branch & Cut?
cards.answer: Combining branching and cutting planes — the engine inside every real MIP solver.

cards.question: What is the dual of an LP?
cards.answer: A mirror-image problem (max becomes min) that puts a ceiling on how good any answer could possibly be.

cards.question: What does the Strong Duality Theorem say?
cards.answer: If the primal has an optimal solution, so does the dual, and their optimal values are exactly equal.

cards.question: What is the 'Most Fractional' branching rule?
cards.answer: Branch on the variable closest to halfway between two integers. Simple, but often mediocre.

cards.question: What is Pseudo-Cost Branching?
cards.answer: Keep a running report card per variable estimating how helpful branching on it usually is. Fast but needs data to warm up.

cards.question: What is the main weakness of Pseudo-Cost Branching?
cards.answer: Scores get better over time, but the earliest choices matter most — exactly when scores are least reliable.

cards.question: What is Strong Branching?
cards.answer: Actually test-solving both children for several candidate variables and picking the best. Accurate but expensive.

cards.question: What is Reliability Branching?
cards.answer: A hybrid: use Strong Branching early, then switch to pseudo-costs once they've gathered enough data to be reliable.

cards.question: Why must solvers manage cuts and sometimes 'forget' them?
cards.answer: Too many cuts slow the LP down, so solvers keep a cut pool and drop cuts that aren't helping.

cards.question: What does Presolve do before the real solving starts?
cards.answer: Cleans up the problem — removing useless variables, tightening bounds, rescaling — often the single biggest speedup.

cards.question: What are primal heuristics and why do they help?
cards.answer: Quick tricks to find good real answers fast, giving the upper bounds needed to prune branches.

cards.question: What's the difference between construction and improvement heuristics?
cards.answer: Construction builds a solution from nothing (e.g. diving, feasibility pump); improvement polishes an existing one (e.g. RINS, local branching).

cards.question: What is the Feasibility Pump idea?
cards.answer: Bounce between the LP answer and the nearest rounded point until they line up on a valid solution.

cards.question: What is the idea behind Local Branching / RINS?
cards.answer: Freeze most of a good solution and let the solver re-optimize only a small piece as a mini sub-problem.

cards.question: Summarize how solvers crack a MIP in one sentence.
cards.answer: Repeatedly solve the easy LP to get bounds, then squeeze the upper and lower bounds together using cuts and branching until they meet.

lectures.title: Tutorial 03 · LP & MIP Modeling
lectures.description: The modeling toolkit — Big-M, semi-continuous, PWL, strong formulations.

cards.question: State the general Big-M construction for gating a linear constraint by a binary, then its main drawback and the mitigations.
cards.answer: To express b = 1 ⇒ aᵀx ≤ c linearly, write aᵀx ≤ c + M(1 − b) with M large enough that b = 0 leaves the constraint vacuous. Disjunctions, implications, on/off costs and fixed charges all fit this one template. The price: it is not LP-preserving. A fractional b already buys most of the M-slack, so the relaxation collapses and the root bound can be near useless — branch & bound has to rebuild the structure node by node. Mitigations: use the smallest valid M, or avoid Big-M via disaggregation / native indicator constraints.

cards.question: Define a semi-continuous variable and give its MIP encoding.
cards.answer: x is either off or inside an operating band: x ∈ {0} ∪ [L, U] with 0 < L ≤ U. Encoding: L·b ≤ x ≤ U·b, b ∈ {0,1}. b = 0 squeezes x between 0 and 0, forcing x = 0; b = 1 forces x ∈ [L, U]. The same b doubles as a fixed-charge switch — add c·b to the objective and you pay c exactly when the unit runs.

cards.question: For x ∈ [L₁,U₁] ∪ [L₂,U₂] ∪ [L₃,U₃], why does reusing one shared x with three semi-continuous blocks fail, and what is the correct encoding?
cards.answer: (a) Each off block independently forces x = 0, so the two intervals you did not pick pin x to zero — the model is only feasible at x = 0. (b) Give each interval its own part and let an exactly-one selector pick the live one: x = x₁ + x₂ + x₃, with Lₖ·bₖ ≤ xₖ ≤ Uₖ·bₖ and Σₖ bₖ = 1. The unselected parts are then zero and contribute nothing to the sum.

cards.question: How do you cast min-max and min-absolute-value objectives as LPs — and why doesn't the trick extend to maximising |x|?
cards.answer: min maxᵢ fᵢ(x) → introduce z: min z s.t. fᵢ(x) ≤ z for all i. min Σ|xᵢ − tᵢ| → introduce dᵢ: min Σ dᵢ s.t. dᵢ ≥ xᵢ − tᵢ and dᵢ ≥ tᵢ − xᵢ. Both are correct only because the objective pushes the auxiliary down until it hits the true max / absolute value — nothing else holds it there. Maximising |x|, or forcing |x| = d, instead needs x pinned to an edge of a non-convex set, which the auxiliary trick cannot do: it takes a binary and Big-M (x = d·b − d·(1−b)).

cards.question: How is a piecewise-linear (PWL) function encoded, and when is PWL approximation especially cheap?
cards.answer: Breakpoints (Xₖ, Yₖ) are data; each point is a convex combination: x = ΣλₖXₖ, y = ΣλₖYₖ, Σλₖ = 1, λ ≥ 0 — plus adjacency (only two consecutive λ may be non-zero), enforced by one activation binary per segment. It can approximate any nonlinear function to the accuracy of your breakpoints. If f is convex and you are minimising, the binaries disappear: the epigraph y ≥ f(x) is convex, so tangent half-planes y ≥ f'(Xₖ)(x − Xₖ) + f(Xₖ) give an LP-only encoding and objective pressure pulls y onto the envelope. Equality y = f(x) still needs the selection binaries.

cards.question: Facility location links xᵢⱼ to yⱼ either disaggregated (xᵢⱼ ≤ yⱼ) or aggregated (Σᵢ xᵢⱼ ≤ n·yⱼ). Same integer solutions — which is better in practice, and why?
cards.answer: Disaggregated wins. Both describe the identical integer feasible set, but branch & bound lives off the LP relaxation. xᵢⱼ ≤ yⱼ forces yⱼ up to the largest single assignment — n·m small, tight inequalities. The aggregated version is a Big-M with M = n: one served customer only requires yⱼ ≥ 1/n, so the opening cost is almost free in the relaxation and the bound is weak. The lesson: many tight constraints beat few loose ones, even though the model is larger.

lectures.title: Lecture 08 · Graph & Network Algorithms
lectures.description: The four classic graph problems — shortest path, matching, flow, and spanning trees — explained through everyday examples.

cards.question: What is special about a graph with no loops (a DAG or tree)?
cards.answer: You can never walk in a circle back to the start, which makes them much easier to solve — often with simple step-by-step dynamic programming.

cards.question: What are the four classic graph problems in this lecture?
cards.answer: Shortest path, matching, flow, and spanning tree (MST).

cards.question: Why prefer a graph algorithm over a general solver when possible?
cards.answer: Graph algorithms are more specialized and much faster than throwing a general-purpose (LP/MIP) solver at the problem.

cards.question: How does Dijkstra's algorithm explore the graph?
cards.answer: Like water flooding outward — it always settles the nearest unvisited node next, in all directions equally.

cards.question: How is A* different from Dijkstra?
cards.answer: A* adds a hint pointing toward the goal, so it searches in a focused cone instead of flooding everywhere.

cards.question: What is the one rule the A* hint must obey?
cards.answer: It must never overestimate the real remaining distance; an honest under-guess keeps A* correct.

cards.question: How can A* solve a sliding tile puzzle with ~180,000 states?
cards.answer: It treats each arrangement as a node and generates the graph as it goes, touching only ~283 states instead of storing them all.

cards.question: Which algorithm fits an unweighted graph?
cards.answer: BFS — just count the number of steps.

cards.question: Which algorithm handles negative edge weights?
cards.answer: Bellman-Ford (plain Dijkstra can fail with negative weights).

cards.question: Why can time-dependent paths make a longer road the better choice?
cards.answer: Travel time depends on when you enter a road, so a longer route can dodge rush hour — sometimes even waiting before leaving helps.

cards.question: Time-dependent shortest paths can still be solved with Dijkstra — under which condition, and what does it mean?
cards.answer: The FIFO or 'no overtaking' condition: arrival arr(t) = t + tt(t) must be non-decreasing in departure time — leaving later can never make you arrive earlier. Then the optimality principle still holds and Dijkstra is unchanged: just evaluate each edge's travel time at the current arrival time. Break it (a bridge that closes, so arr(t) dips) and waiting before you leave can pay off — Dijkstra's greedy settle is then invalid.

cards.question: Why is the resource-constrained shortest path surprisingly hard?
cards.answer: Finding the cheapest path that also respects a limit (like a fuel budget) is NP-hard — the cheapest path is often illegal, forcing a pricier legal one.

cards.question: What is a matching in a graph?
cards.answer: A set of edges where no two edges share a node — everyone gets at most one partner.

cards.question: Name and define the two main variants of maximum matching.
cards.answer: Maximum cardinality — the largest possible set of disjoint edges (ties often broken by minimum total weight: largest matching of minimum weight). Maximum weight — edges carry weights wₑ and you maximise Σ wₑ. They are genuinely different: a max-weight matching need not be cardinality-maximal — a smaller matching can be worth more. Two things help in practice: integral weights are more robust than fractional ones, and bipartite graphs admit faster algorithms.

cards.question: What is the assignment problem?
cards.answer: A minimum-cost perfect matching — assign each order to one machine so the total cost is lowest.

cards.question: What makes matching harder to solve?
cards.answer: Odd cycles — bipartite graphs (no odd cycles) allow clean, faster algorithms.

cards.question: What does the max flow problem ask?
cards.answer: How much can you push from a source to a sink through pipes that each have a capacity limit?

cards.question: What limits the total flow through a network?
cards.answer: The bottleneck — the tightest pipe or cut along the way.

cards.question: How is min-cost flow different from max flow?
cards.answer: Each pipe charges a fee per unit, so the goal is to move the required flow at the least total cost.

cards.question: Why is the residual graph the key idea in flow algorithms?
cards.answer: It lets the algorithm undo or reroute earlier flow, so bad early decisions can be fixed to reach the true optimum.

cards.question: Why is min-cost flow called a 'Swiss Army knife'?
cards.answer: Shortest path, assignment, and transportation problems are all special cases of it.

cards.question: When does a flow problem stop being easy?
cards.answer: With multiple goods sharing pipes or costs to open an edge — then it becomes NP-hard and needs a MIP solver.

cards.question: What is a minimum spanning tree (MST)?
cards.answer: The cheapest set of edges that connects every node with no wasteful loops.

cards.question: Name the three greedy algorithms that build an MST.
cards.answer: Kruskal, Prim, and Borůvka — all reach the same optimum.

cards.question: Why is greedy special for the MST?
cards.answer: For the MST, always grabbing the cheapest safe edge is provably exact — rare, since greedy usually isn't optimal.

cards.question: What is the practical performance warning about matching and flow?
cards.answer: They usually run fast, but their roughly cubic worst case can become critical on very large or tricky inputs.

lectures.title: Lecture 09 · SAT
lectures.description: How SAT solvers work: from DPLL guessing to modern CDCL learning.

cards.question: In plain words, what does the SAT problem ask?
cards.answer: Can we set a bunch of true/false switches (variables) so that every rule is satisfied at the same time? If yes it's satisfiable, if impossible it's unsatisfiable.

cards.question: What is a 'clause' in SAT?
cards.answer: A single rule that says at least one of these options must be true (an OR-list of literals). The whole formula requires ALL clauses to hold at once.

cards.question: What's the difference between a variable and a literal?
cards.answer: A variable is a yes/no switch; a literal is that switch or its opposite (the switch, or 'not' the switch).

cards.question: Why is SAT historically important?
cards.answer: It was the first problem ever proven NP-complete — the 'granddaddy' of hard problems. A fast trick for SAT would crack a huge family of hard problems.

cards.question: What is DPLL, at a high level?
cards.answer: The classic solver that works like Sudoku: guess, fill in all forced moves, and backtrack (undo) when you hit a contradiction.

cards.question: What is Unit Propagation, and why does it matter?
cards.answer: When a clause shrinks to only one option left, you're forced to pick it — which can force more moves in a domino chain. It's the single most important and most-used move.

cards.question: What is the Pure Literal rule?
cards.answer: If a variable only ever appears in one form (always 'on' or always 'off'), just set it that way for free — it can only help. Removes all clauses it satisfies.

cards.question: SAT only answers yes/no. So how do you use a SAT solver to optimize something?
cards.answer: Restate the optimization problem as a sequence of decision problems: 'is there a solution with objective ≤ k?' Then search over k — binary search, or ratchet k down each time you succeed — until the answer flips to UNSAT, which proves the last feasible k optimal. Example (bottleneck crossing-free matching): guess a maximum edge length L, forbid every edge longer than L, and ask SAT whether a crossing-free perfect matching still exists; binary-search L over the O(n²) candidate edge lengths. Use the solver incrementally — adding clauses is cheap and learned clauses are kept between calls.

cards.question: What is the 'trail' in a SAT solver?
cards.answer: An ordered stack of everything set true so far, split into decision levels. Level 0 is true forever; each new guess starts a new level.

cards.question: How does backtracking work with the trail?
cards.answer: You just chop off the top decision level — cheap, like tearing the last page off a notepad.

cards.question: What are the invariants of the Two Watched Literals scheme?
cards.answer: Always watch two non-false literals (true or still open) in every clause. For a satisfied clause it is enough to watch the true literal plus any other, even a false one. The pay-off: while two literals are non-false the clause cannot be unit, so unit propagation may ignore it entirely. When a watched literal becomes false you search that clause for a replacement; if there is none, the clause is unit (forcing the other watched literal) or, if that one is false too, a conflict.

cards.question: What must you fix about watched literals when you backtrack? Why?
cards.answer: Nothing! Undoing can only bring dead switches back to life, never kill live ones, so 'watch two live options' stays true automatically. Doing nothing is the cheapest option.

cards.question: What is DPLL's big weakness?
cards.answer: It never learns from its mistakes — it wanders back into the same hidden dead-ends over and over, which can make it insanely slow.

cards.question: What is resolution, in simple terms?
cards.answer: A way to combine two rules into a new true rule by cancelling out one variable: 'A or X' plus 'B or not-X' gives you 'A or B'.

cards.question: How can resolution prove a formula is unsatisfiable?
cards.answer: Keep combining rules until you reach the empty clause (a rule with zero options = flat-out impossible). That proves the whole thing is UNSAT.

cards.question: What is CDCL and why is it the modern standard?
cards.answer: Conflict-Driven Clause Learning: it's DPLL that learns a new rule from every crash, so it never repeats the same mistake.

cards.question: What extra info does each forced (propagated) literal store?
cards.answer: Its reason — the clause that forced it. These reasons are traced back to build the learned clause when a conflict happens.

cards.question: What happens on a conflict in CDCL?
cards.answer: You trace backwards through the reasons using resolution to find the real cause, distill it into a new learned rule, and add it to the rulebook forever.

cards.question: What is backjumping?
cards.answer: Instead of undoing one step, leap far back to the earliest level where the newly learned rule would have helped — skipping useless branches at once.

cards.question: What is the First UIP (Unique Implication Point)?
cards.answer: The single bottleneck moment in the current level that all the trouble funnels through. Learning the rule right there gives the tightest, most useful lesson.

cards.question: Why is CDCL guaranteed to terminate?
cards.answer: Every conflict teaches a genuinely new rule (never a repeat), and there are only finitely many possible rules to learn — so it must finish.

cards.question: What are the two ways a CDCL run can end?
cards.answer: Either fill in every switch with no conflict (SAT — answer found), or learn the empty clause (UNSAT — proven impossible).

cards.question: What does VSIDS do?
cards.answer: A variable-picking heuristic: prefer switches recently involved in many conflicts — the 'spicy' important ones. Like a firefighter rushing to where fires keep flaring.

cards.question: What are restarts, and why keep doing them?
cards.answer: Throw away all current guesses and start fresh, but keep everything learned (clauses + scores). Stops the solver getting stuck in a bad part of the search.

cards.question: What is phase saving?
cards.answer: When you undo a switch, remember which way it was set and try that same value next time — so you don't re-discover good settings you already found.

cards.question: Why delete learned clauses, and how do we choose which?
cards.answer: The rulebook bloats and slows down, so we toss the least useful rules. The best quality measure is the glue score (LBD): rules spanning fewer decision levels are stickier and worth keeping.

cards.question: What is conflict clause minimization?
cards.answer: After learning a rule, trim redundant parts so it's shorter and punchier — shorter rules are faster and more powerful.

cards.question: What is pre-/in-processing, and its most useful technique?
cards.answer: Cleaning up and simplifying the rulebook before and during solving. The single most effective is Bounded Variable Elimination (BVE): remove a variable by folding its clauses together, if it doesn't blow up.

cards.question: What must a preprocessing technique preserve to be sound — and what is the weaker property that suffices?
cards.answer: The strong property is logical equivalence: the formula has exactly the same models before and after. But that is more than you need — it suffices that the technique is equisatisfiable: the new formula is satisfiable if and only if the old one is, even if the set of models changes. That weaker bar is what lets techniques like bounded variable elimination work — BVE deletes a variable entirely, so the models cannot be the same, yet any model of the result extends to a model of the original. To argue soundness you show both directions: every model of the original survives, and every model of the result can be extended back.

cards.question: What are SAT/UNSAT stages?
cards.answer: Finding an answer vs. proving none exists need different styles, so the solver switches its personality depending on which case it thinks it's in.

cards.question: What is local search (probSAT/YalSAT) used for here?
cards.answer: A 'wander and fix' method that flips switches to satisfy more rules, run on the side to suggest good settings to the main CDCL engine.

cards.question: What does the lecture say to focus on for the exam?
cards.answer: CDCL, the trail & decision levels, conflict resolution (learning + backjumping), and the high-level ideas behind the tricks — not every optimization's inner details.

lectures.title: Tutorial 04 · Real-World Optimization Problems
lectures.description: Three families that cover most of industry: shop scheduling, rostering, routing.

cards.question: Describe Job-Shop Scheduling (JSP) informally, then name at least three constraint or objective variants that occur in practice.
cards.answer: Each job is a chain of tasks that must run in order; each task needs a particular machine, and a machine does one thing at a time. A solution is a Gantt chart: for every task, which resource, when it starts, when it ends. Practical add-ons: sequence-dependent setup times, no-wait / blocking (no buffer between steps), release and due dates (so the objective becomes tardiness rather than plain makespan), resource breaks and time windows, multi-mode tasks and consumables.

cards.question: Name at least three structural variants of the job-shop topology (not constraint add-ons) and how each differs from classical JSP.
cards.answer: Flexible job shop: a task may run on any of several eligible machines, possibly with different durations — you now also choose which machine. Flow shop: every job visits the machines in the same order, so only the entry order is left to decide (jobs may still overtake at later stages). Permutation flow shop: flow shop plus no overtaking — one permutation holds everywhere, a far smaller space but still NP-hard from three stages. Hybrid flow shop: each stage has a pool of parallel machines. Open shop: each job visits each machine once but the order is free. RCPSP: tasks consume a quantity of a resource instead of blocking a whole machine.

cards.question: Describe employee rostering, then name at least three constraint/objective families — typically hard or soft?
cards.answer: Allocate people to shifts over a horizon; the output is a roster matrix (employee × day → shift or off). Hard (violation = infeasible, and often legal): coverage min/max and skill mix, rest periods and working-time limits, contracts and availability. Soft (allowed but penalised): personal preferences, fairness across nights/weekends/holidays, schedule stability. Solvers compare a multi-level (hard, medium, soft) score lexicographically — hard must be zero before soft is even looked at. What makes it its own problem: people are not interchangeable, the hard constraints are law, and fairness is a first-class objective.

cards.question: Describe the core Vehicle Routing Problem, then name at least three practical variants from the lecture.
cards.answer: One depot, a set of clients, a fleet of vehicles: visit every client exactly once, every route starts and ends at the depot, minimise total cost. Variants: capacity (CVRP) — without it one vehicle would do everything, so capacity is what determines fleet size; time windows (VRPTW) — clients aren't available all day, coupling the visit sequence to the clock; pickups & deliveries; driver shifts (earliest start, latest end, overtime band — usually soft); hours-of-service breaks (45 min after 4.5 h driving, whose placement is itself a decision); multiple depots; heterogeneous fleet with per-vehicle distance matrices.

lectures.title: Lecture 10 · CP-SAT Technology & Explainability
lectures.description: How the CP-SAT solver works inside: clever deduction plus learning from mistakes.

cards.question: In one sentence, what is CP-SAT?
cards.answer: A puzzle solver that combines clever deduction (CP) with learning from mistakes (SAT) — 'a CP solver with a SAT solver's memory'.

cards.question: What kind of problems does CP-SAT solve?
cards.answer: Problems with boxes to fill using whole numbers and a list of rules (constraints) — it finds an assignment satisfying every rule, or proves none exists. (Think Sudoku for real-world scheduling, routing, planning.)

cards.question: What is CP's superpower?
cards.answer: Smart deduction (propagation): ruling out impossible options because the rules force it, like a good Sudoku player — no guessing needed.

cards.question: What is SAT's (CDCL's) superpower?
cards.answer: Learning from failure: every time it hits a dead end, it writes a permanent 'never do this again' note, so it never repeats the same mistake.

cards.question: What weakness of classic CP does CP-SAT fix?
cards.answer: Classic CP is a great deducer but forgetful — it backtracks and forgets why it failed. CP-SAT adds SAT-style memory so failures become lasting lessons.

cards.question: How does CP-SAT store a number while solving?
cards.answer: Not as a fixed value, but as a shrinking range (a lowest and a highest it could be). Like the 'higher or lower' guessing game.

cards.question: When is a variable finally 'solved'?
cards.answer: When its low end and high end squeeze together into a single number. Until then, it has no single value.

cards.question: What does a propagator do?
cards.answer: A helper attached to each rule that squeezes ranges tighter whenever the rule forces it — and can trigger other propagators like falling dominoes.

cards.question: Does propagation ever guess?
cards.answer: No. It only states things that are 100% forced by the current ranges. When forced moves run out, the solver must make a guess instead.

cards.question: What is a 'conflict'?
cards.answer: When squeezing leaves a box with no numbers left — a dead end. It sounds bad but is actually useful, because it's what the solver learns from.

cards.question: What extra duty does a CP-SAT propagator have that classic CP lacks?
cards.answer: It must be able to explain every deduction — name the exact facts (bounds) that forced it — like a detective showing the evidence, not just naming the culprit.

cards.question: Why must propagators be able to explain themselves?
cards.answer: Because to learn from a mistake, the solver needs to know exactly which facts caused it. No reason = nothing to write in the 'dead ends' notebook.

cards.question: What does 'lazy' mean for explanations?
cards.answer: The full explanation is built only when it's actually needed — i.e. when that deduction turns out to be part of a dead end — not up front.

cards.question: Why is being lazy worthwhile?
cards.answer: Most deductions never cause trouble, so explaining all of them up front would be wasted effort. You only pay for the reasons you truly use (like homework done when it's due).

cards.question: When CP-SAT hits a dead end, what does it do instead of just undoing the last guess?
cards.answer: It traces the reasons back to find the few guesses truly to blame, learns a permanent rule against that combo, and jumps back (backjumps) past the innocent guesses in one leap.

cards.question: What is a 'nogood'?
cards.answer: The learned rule saved after a conflict — 'never combine these facts again' — so the solver never repeats that mistake.

cards.question: What is CP-SAT's one big unifying idea?
cards.answer: Lazy clause generation: propagators that both squeeze ranges AND explain themselves, so the SAT memory can learn from every CP deduction. It's the glue joining the two halves.

cards.question: How does CP-SAT decide what to guess?
cards.answer: With VSIDS: it guesses on whatever has been causing the most recent conflicts — focusing on the troublemakers.

cards.question: Why can't VSIDS finish the puzzle alone, and what closes the gap?
cards.answer: VSIDS only settles simple yes/no facts, which can leave a number still un-pinned. Integer completion then nails down each leftover range to one value.

cards.question: What accelerators are bolted onto the core?
cards.answer: LP relaxation (fraction-based hints from the MIP world), cutting planes, restarts (wipe guesses but keep lessons), and a parallel portfolio of solvers that share what they learn.

lectures.title: Tutorial 05 · Benchmarking & Parameter Tuning
lectures.description: Timeouts, variance, and picking a number that doesn't lie.

cards.question: Why is benchmarking optimization solvers fundamentally hard? Name at least three reasons.
cards.answer: 1. Runtimes vary by orders of magnitude on NP-hard instances — a trend may exist, but no single instance settles who wins. 2. Too few instances, unevenly spread — they cluster and leave gaps, so a trend line across a gap is wishful interpolation. 3. No clean x-axis: a SAT formula's hardness isn't captured by variable and clause counts — SATzilla needs dozens of features, so there is no simple scalability plot. 4. No clean y-axis either — you are comparing time and solution quality at once, inherently multi-objective. 5. Timeouts leave genuine unknowns you cannot fill in honestly.

cards.question: Timeouts produce censored data. What does that mean, why does the way you book them change the conclusion, and which metric addresses it?
cards.answer: A timeout tells you only that the run took at least the limit — you never learn whether it was 1 s from done or stuck forever. So the booking decides the result: removing timeouts silently drops the hard instances (flattering the weaker solver), substituting the limit under-estimates, and 'no time limit' means running forever. Either way you over- or under-estimate the mean. PAR2 / PAR10 addresses it by charging a fixed penalty of 2× or 10× the time limit per timeout, which keeps every instance in the average. Caveat: the penalty factor is arbitrary — it ranks solvers rather than measuring them.

cards.question: Which 'average' should you report over a benchmark set, and what do integral metrics add?
cards.answer: Runtimes are right-skewed and often censored, so match the summary to that shape. Arithmetic mean: one hard instance drags it into the tail, and a timeout leaves nothing finite to average. Median + IQR: robust to outliers and censoring, but silent about the tail. Geometric mean: the only honest way to average ratios and speedups. p90 / p95: describe the tail the median hides. Integral metrics (primal / dual / primal-dual integral — area under the gap curve) score the whole anytime trajectory rather than one instant, so a config that closes the gap earlier wins even when time-to-optimality ties.

lectures.title: Lecture 11 · Decomposition
lectures.description: Breaking a big, tangled optimization problem into small easy pieces held together by a little glue.

cards.question: What is the core idea of Lagrangian relaxation?
cards.answer: Don't forbid breaking a rule — charge a price (penalty) for breaking it. Move the annoying constraint into the objective as a fee, then adjust the fee until the solver stops cheating on its own. (Like library late fees.)

cards.question: What problem does column generation solve, and how?
cards.answer: There are astronomically many patterns to list. Instead of listing them all, generate patterns lazily — start with a few and only invent a new one when it would actually improve the answer.

cards.question: What is the core idea of Benders' decomposition?
cards.answer: A master picks the hard strategic choices with an optimistic cost guess; a subproblem fills in the easy details and reports back with cuts that correct the guess. (Pick the cities, let a helper compute the real route.)

lectures.title: Lecture 12 · Metaheuristics
lectures.description: Clever tricks for finding good-enough answers fast when perfect solutions take too long.

cards.question: What is a local optimum, and why is it the big problem in local search?
cards.answer: A solution where every small neighboring move looks worse, so the search stops — but it may only be the bottom of a small dip, not the true best answer. It traps simple hill-climbing.

cards.question: What is the key idea of Simulated Annealing, and where does it come from?
cards.answer: Inspired by slowly cooling hot metal. A high 'temperature' early on allows accepting worse moves (to escape traps); as it cools, it accepts worse moves less and less until it settles into a good solution.

cards.question: How does Ruin & Recreate / Large Neighborhood Search (LNS) work?
cards.answer: Destroy a chunk of a good solution, then rebuild that chunk smarter. This explores a huge neighborhood at once, and the repair step can even use an exact solver on the small piece.

cards.question: What are the main steps of a Genetic Algorithm?
cards.answer: Keep a population, score each by fitness, select mostly-fit parents, crossover them into children, apply small random mutations, and repeat over generations. Diversity keeps exploration strong.

lectures.title: Lecture 13 · Trade-offs and Uncertainty
lectures.description: Handling problems with many goals at once and futures we can't predict.

cards.question: What two things does the standard 'minimize one number, all facts known' model ignore about real problems?
cards.answer: That quality is usually multidimensional (you care about several goals at once) and that the world is uncertain (you don't know exactly what will happen).

cards.question: What is a 'trade-off' between objectives?
cards.answer: When improving one goal makes another worse, like making ice cream healthier but less tasty. There's often no single 'best' answer.

cards.question: When can you say one option 'dominates' another?
cards.answer: When it is at least as good on every goal and strictly better on at least one. A dominated option is beaten on everything, so you can safely throw it out.

cards.question: What is the Pareto front, and what's the catch about it?
cards.answer: The set of 'survivor' options that nothing else beats on every goal. The catch: being on the front doesn't make an option good, only 'not clearly beaten' — a boring option can still survive.

cards.question: Which two questions must you keep separate in multi-objective problems?
cards.answer: How do we decide which solution is better (the comparison rules) versus how do we find good solutions (the search). Like deciding what's for dinner vs. actually cooking it.

cards.question: What is the 'weighted sum' approach, and its main danger?
cards.answer: You give each goal a weight and add them into one score. The danger: the weights act like secret exchange rates (e.g. '1 changeover = 5 minutes late') that someone must justify — and different units can trick you.

cards.question: What is lexicographic (priority-ladder) optimization?
cards.answer: You handle the most important goal first, then the next, never trading away a top goal for a lower one — like always packing the baby's diapers before trying to pack light.

cards.question: What is the ε-constraint (limits) approach?
cards.answer: You optimize one goal while forcing the others to stay under fixed limits, e.g. 'make the trip cheapest, but under 2 hours and at most 1 transfer.'

cards.question: When can't you name your preferences up front — and what do you do instead?
cards.answer: Often people only know what they want after seeing the options. So compute a menu of good alternatives (extremes, balanced middles, and 'knee' sweet spots) and let a human pick.

cards.question: What does the sandwich shop (newsvendor) story teach?
cards.answer: You must decide before you know the truth, and because running out hurts more than leftovers, you should prepare a bit more than the average demand. The cost of each mistake shapes your choice.

cards.question: Why is predicting just the 'average future' a trap?
cards.answer: A single best guess throws away how uncertain things are — the exact info the decision needs. Even a perfect average prediction can lead to a bad decision.

cards.question: What three questions should you ask before modeling uncertainty?
cards.answer: What is uncertain, what do we know about it, and when is it revealed (can we still adapt afterward)?

cards.question: What is the idea of robust optimization, and its downside?
cards.answer: Make a plan that survives every plausible 'world' at once, like packing umbrella, sunscreen and jacket. Downside: guarding against everything going wrong together is overly pessimistic and costly.

cards.question: Why is a 'budget' of uncertainty smarter than protecting against the absolute worst?
cards.answer: Bad luck tends to cancel out (like coin flips rarely being all heads), so you protect against a realistic amount of trouble instead of an impossible all-at-once disaster. Safety is a dial, not a switch.

cards.question: How does stochastic optimization differ from robust optimization?
cards.answer: Instead of only fearing the worst, it weighs each possible future by how likely it is and aims for the best result on average (like planning lemonade around cold/normal/hot day odds).

cards.question: What is 'recourse' (two-stage decisions), and the golden rule that goes with it?
cards.answer: Decide what you must now, and leave flexible reactions for after you learn more (like hiring staff now, calling extra help later). Golden rule: no crystal ball — your 'now' choice can't peek at the future.

cards.question: Why isn't optimizing for the 'average' automatically safe?
cards.answer: Chasing the best average can quietly accept a rare disaster because it's unlikely. If a bad outcome would be catastrophic, you need extra rules to limit the risky tail, and always test plans on fresh scenarios.

lectures.title: Tutorial 06 · Coding Patterns, TDD & Debugging
lectures.description: When it's UNSAT, ask for a small subset — not a verdict on everything.

cards.question: Define a Minimal Unsatisfiable Subset (MUS).
cards.answer: A subset C' ⊆ C that is both (a) unsatisfiable on its own — solve(C') = UNSAT — and (b) minimal: removing any single constraint c ∈ C' makes it satisfiable. It converts the useless verdict 'all your constraints together are infeasible' into one pinpointed conflict. Same object, different vocabulary: MIP solvers call it an IIS (irreducible infeasible subset).

cards.question: Define a Minimal Correction Subset (MCS) and contrast it with a MUS.
cards.answer: A minimal set of constraints whose removal makes the model satisfiable — the complement of a maximal satisfiable subset. It is the counterfactual view: a MUS answers 'which constraints fight each other?', while an MCS answers 'what do I drop to fix it?' In the 2-colouring example the MUS is the odd triangle; the MCS is the single edge you delete to make the graph 2-colourable.

lectures.title: Lecture 14 · Analytics Lifecycle & Sequential Decisions
lectures.description: Why the model is the small part, and why repeating decisions need a policy, not a plan.

cards.question: What is the single most important message of the whole lecture?
cards.answer: The clever math (the model) is the small, easy part. Projects succeed or fail in the framing before it and the deployment and upkeep after it.

cards.question: What are the two 'lenses' the lecture gives you?
cards.answer: The Project Lens (how a whole project runs, start to finish) and the Time Lens (how to decide again and again as new surprises arrive).

cards.question: What are the three kinds of analytics, and how do they stack?
cards.answer: Descriptive (what happened?), Predictive (what will happen?), and Prescriptive (what should we do?). They form a stack, not a menu: doing rests on predicting, which rests on describing.

cards.question: How much of a real project is actually building the model, and why does that matter?
cards.answer: Only about 10–15% of the time. It shows the math is rarely the stage that decides success.

cards.question: What are the 7 stages of the INFORMS Analytics Framework?
cards.answer: Business Problem Framing, Analytics Problem Framing, Data, Methodology, Model Building, Deployment, and Lifecycle Management. Model Building is just stage 5 of 7.

cards.question: Why is the framework called a 'cogwheel, not a pipeline'?
cards.answer: The stages loop backward as often as forward. A discovery late (like missing data) can invalidate an assumption made early, forcing you to walk back and re-frame.

cards.question: Where do projects actually die, and is it a technical problem?
cards.answer: At Deployment, and it's mostly organizational, not technical. A plan people don't trust, no fallback for a bad night, or no internal champion kills it.

cards.question: If you only assess three risks before a project, which three matter most?
cards.answer: Decision Scope (state it in one sentence?), Value Proposition (worth in real money per year?), and Process Maturity (has this org shipped such a system before?).

cards.question: In the nurse-ward story, the math was perfect and data honest — so why did the roster fail?
cards.answer: The horizon was the problem. No fixed two-week plan survives two weeks of surprises, because the surprises (like sick calls) arrive after you've committed.

cards.question: What is the key difference between a plan and a policy?
cards.answer: A plan is a fixed sequence chosen up front, optimal for only one imagined future. A policy is a rule from 'what you know now' to a decision, so it works for every future because it consumes new information as it arrives.

cards.question: What three questions must you answer to frame a sequential-decision problem?
cards.answer: What are the decisions and who owns them? How do we measure quality? What uncertainty affects performance? (Frame it before reaching for a method.)

cards.question: What does 'SDA' stand for, and what gap does it address?
cards.answer: Sequential Decision Analytics. A one-shot optimization model answers 'what is the best plan for this snapshot?' — but real decisions repeat as new information arrives (sick calls, admissions, cancellations). SDA is the framing for that stream: instead of one plan you design a policy, then tune it against many plausible futures in a simulator.

cards.question: What are the five elements of a sequential decision problem?
cards.answer: 1. State — everything you know right now (commitments, who is out, forecast, fairness and hours ledgers). 2. Decision — what you may choose, and its feasible set. 3. Exogenous information — what the world reveals between decisions (sick calls, admissions, whether offered overtime is accepted). 4. Transition — how state + decision + new information produce the next state (overtime tonight rest-locks a nurse tomorrow). 5. Objective — what you score over time (labour + shortfall + fairness + stability). Warren Powell's framing: write these down before reaching for a method.

cards.question: Why measure quality with several conflicting numbers instead of one weighted sum?
cards.answer: A single sum hides what you give up. Cost, coverage, fairness, preferences, stability, and tail safety pull against each other — surface the trade-offs instead.

cards.question: What is the 'simulator', and what is it NOT?
cards.answer: It's a wind tunnel, not a forecast. It doesn't predict who gets sick next Tuesday; it shows how a policy behaves across many plausible futures, so you can tune safely without experimenting on real people.

cards.question: Why score a policy on the worst nights instead of the average night?
cards.answer: Being short-staffed hurts far more than being spare, and the optimizer amplifies forecast errors by pushing against constraints. So you plan for the bad tail, not the comfortable mean.

cards.question: What are Powell's four families of policies, and does fancier mean better?
cards.answer: PFA (a tuned rule), CFA (solve a fudged proxy goal), VFA (learn a state's future worth), DLA (simulate ahead). More complex is not better — a fancy policy can be too slow for the huddle and too opaque to trust.

cards.question: Why write down the nurses' by-hand routine as the baseline?
cards.answer: Their routine actually worked better in reality, so it becomes the honest baseline anything fancier must beat — not a strawman. Often the rule was right and only the cutoff needed tuning.
